PLAINTIFF STEVEN BRUCE'S MOTION FOR RECONSIDERATION OF ORDER GRANTING DEFENDANT'S MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION (TENTATIVE RULING PART 2 OF 2)
Off Calendar Jul-30-2025 Off Calendar

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Action
Off Calendar Jul-01-2025 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT JAGUAR LAND ROVER NORTH AMERICA's Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Action.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

The motion to compel arbitration and stay proceedings is granted.

California courts have adopted a three-step process shifting the burden of producing evidence as to the existence of an arbitration agreement. (Ramirez v. Golden Queen Mining Company, LLC (2024) 102 Cal.App.5th 821, 830; Iyere v. Wise Auto Group (2023) 87 Cal.App.5th 747, 755.) The first step requires Defendant to present "prima facie evidence of a written agreement to arbitrate the controversy." (Ramirez, supra, 102 Cal.App.5th at p. 830.) If Defendant meets this initial burden, the second step requires Plaintiff to identify a factual dispute as to the agreement's existence and present admissible evidence to support the existence of that factual dispute. (Id. at p. 832.) If Plaintiff meets his burden, the third step requires Defendant "to prove by a preponderance of the evidence that the parties formed a valid contract to arbitrate their dispute." (Id. at p. 830.)

Defendant has met its burden of providing prima facie evidence of a written agreement to arbitrate: it has presented evidence that Plaintiff was presented with the owner's handbook for his Land Rover when he bought it from Defendant, and the owner's handbook included an arbitration clause and a prominent statement that use of the vehicle or acceptance of warranty benefits indicated agreement to be bound by the dispute resolution terms, unless the buyer sent an email opting out of arbitration. (Marshall Dec. para. 3 & Ex. A, pp. 416 & 418.)

Plaintiff's opposition to the motion does not provide any admissible evidence showing that no such agreement existed. The Bruce Declaration states at paragraph 5 that "[t]here never was an arbitration agreement" [emphasis omitted], but this is a conclusion and not evidentiary support for the conclusion. It presents nothing for the court to weigh or consider. Moreover, Defendant presents contrary circumstantial evidence that Plaintiff in fact received the arbitration clause and agreed to be bound thereto, to wit that Plaintiff complied with the pre-arbitration dispute resolution provisions spelled out in the handbook. Accordingly, Plaintiff has not rebutted the Defendant's showing of the existence of an agreement, his burden at step two of the Ramirez inquiry. The court does not reach step three of the Ramirez inquiry because Plaintiff has failed to meet his burden at step two.

The motion is granted and this matter is stayed pursuant to CCP 1281.4 pending arbitration.

Order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)
